title: Coordinated Bot Ticketing Rush Using Compromised Card Portfolio
narrative: Fraudsters deploy automated bots to mass-purchase event tickets within minutes of on-sale time, using 15-40 stolen cards from the same BIN range. Transactions cluster in 90-180 second bursts targeting high-demand concerts or sporting events, typically $185-$425 per ticket with 2-4 tickets per transaction. Cards show zero prior purchase history with the merchant (MID). All transactions originate from 2-3 residential proxy IP addresses with identical user-agent strings and ProductCD='C' for entertainment. Email domains follow auto-generated patterns (gmail accounts created same week). The operation completes before fraud detection triggers, with tickets immediately listed on resale platforms. Chargebacks surface 12-18 days post-event when cardholders notice unauthorized charges.
indicators: 15-40 unique card1 values within 90-180 second window, all targeting same event/ProductCD
TransactionAmt concentrated $185-$425 per ticket, 2-4 quantity, total $370-$1700 per card
card1_txn_count_lifetime = 1 for all cards (first-time use at merchant)
2-3 distinct addr1 (IP addresses) with identical device signatures across entire card set
P_emaildomain shows gmail/yahoo accounts with creation timestamps within 72 hours of transaction
All transactions occur within 3 minutes of event inventory release timestamp
dist1 (distance between billing/IP) exceeds 500km for 80%+ of transactions
summary: Within 90-180 seconds of ticket sales opening, 15-40 different stolen cards from the same bank all purchase the exact same event tickets through 2-3 IP addresses using identical device fingerprints, with every card being used for the first time at this merchant.
reasoning: Fraudsters use bots to scalp high-demand tickets with a portfolio of compromised cards before security systems can react, then immediately resell the tickets while the real cardholders remain unaware until chargebacks appear weeks later.
indicator_explanations: Large batch of different cards hits identical target within tight time window
Purchase amounts cluster in typical ticket price range with consistent quantities
Every card in the burst is making its first purchase ever
Multiple cards share identical device fingerprints from just a few IP addresses
Email accounts were created days before attack suggesting pre-planned coordination
Attack synchronized precisely with high-demand inventory becoming available for sale
Billing addresses don't match cardholder locations shown by IP geolocation
